title: Ego Defense Mechanisms
subtitle: The nurse recognizes defense mechanisms — unconscious ways people cope with stress and anxiety, such as denial, projection, displacement, and rationalization — and knows when they become unhealthy.

patient.name: Defense Mechanisms
patient.admitDate: The nurse identifies how a patient copes with stress and anxiety.

vitals.temp: Shifting feelings to a safe target

labs.v: Shifting emotion to a safe target.
Offsetting a weakness with a strength.

nursesNote: The nurse recognizes ego defense mechanisms, the unconscious mental strategies people use to reduce anxiety and protect the self from stress. Common examples include denial (refusing to accept a painful reality, such as ignoring a new diagnosis), projection (attributing one's own unacceptable feelings to someone else), displacement (shifting an emotion from its source onto a safer target, like snapping at family instead of a boss), rationalization (offering a logical-sounding excuse for behavior), regression (returning to an earlier developmental behavior, common during illness or in children), repression (unconsciously blocking a distressing memory), compensation (offsetting a perceived weakness by excelling in another area), and sublimation (channeling an unacceptable urge into a socially acceptable activity). Defense mechanisms are normal and can be adaptive when used briefly to get through stress; sublimation is generally the healthiest. They become unhealthy when overused, when they distort reality, or when they block problem-solving and daily function. The nurse identifies the mechanism without judging, builds trust, and gently helps the patient develop healthier coping, referring for counseling when defenses interfere with care or safety.
providerOrders: Identify the defense mechanism the patient is using
Build trust and avoid confronting denial harshly early on
Assess whether the mechanism is helping or blocking coping
Support healthier coping skills as the patient is ready
Refer for counseling when defenses impair function or safety

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about defense mechanisms?

opts.text: They are unconscious ways of coping with anxiety
They can be adaptive when used briefly
Denial is refusing to accept a painful reality
They are always chosen consciously and on purpose
Overuse can distort reality and block coping
Sublimation is generally a healthy mechanism
The nurse identifies the mechanism without judging
Harshly confronting denial early builds trust
Regression is common during illness
Displacement shifts feelings to a safer target
Defenses that block function may need referral
Projection means accepting blame for oneself

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order helping a patient stuck in denial of a diagnosis

opts.text: Build a trusting, nonjudgmental relationship
Assess the patient's current understanding
Allow expression of feelings without forcing reality
Gently provide facts as the patient is ready
Support healthier coping and refer if needed
Insisting 'I am not sick' after a clear diagnosis
Yelling at family after a hard talk with the doctor
Accusing others of being angry when you are angry
Saying 'I failed because the test was unfair'
Refusing to discuss a terminal prognosis at all
Blaming a missed dose on the busy schedule

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient displaces anger and snaps at the nurse
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay calm and avoid taking it personally
Acknowledge the patient's feelings
Use open questions to explore the real stressor
Help the patient name healthier coping options
Document the interaction and response
Channeling grief into volunteering (sublimation)
Denying a diagnosis for months and refusing care
Briefly using humor to get through a scary test
Constantly projecting blame so no problem is faced
Compensating for a weakness by building a strength
Rationalizing every harmful choice to avoid change
Brief regression while acutely ill, then recovering
Displacement leading to repeated outbursts at staff
